content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 24, 1991


David Roberti
California State Senator
State Capitol, Room 205
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-91-292

Dear Senator Roberti:

We have received your letter of May 16, 1991, in which you seek advice regarding your duties and responsibilities under the contribution and reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").¹

Because your letter asks several hypothetical questions we are treating it as a request for informal assistance only, pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).²

QUESTION

Does a contribution to you or one of your controlled committees result when:

(1) You discuss pending litigation concerning Propositions 73 or 140 with members of an organization which is a named party in the litigation, and that organization and/or individual members of the organization thereafter make payments directly to the law firm representing your position in the case?

(2) You discuss pending litigation concerning Propositions 73 or 140 with members of an organization which is not a named party in the litigation but which stands to benefit from the

_________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the government code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-292
Page 2


litigation, and that organization and/or individual members of the
organization thereafter make payments directly to the law firm
representing your position in the case?

(3) You discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 73
or 140 with members of an organization which is not a party named
in the litigation but which stands to benefit from the litigation,
and that organization and/or individual members of the
organization thereafter convince person to make payments
directly to the law firm representing your position in the case?

(4) You discuss pending litigation concerning Propositions
73 or 140 with other officeholders who are named as parties in the
litigation, and those officeholders subsequently make payments
directly to the law firm representing your position in the case?

(5) You discuss pending litigation concerning Propositions
73 or 140 with other officeholders who are not parties named in
the litigation but who stand to benefit from the litigation, and
those officeholders subsequently make payments directly to the law
firm representing your position in the case?

(6) You discuss pending litigation concerning Propositions
73 and 140 with other officeholders who are not parties named in
the litigation but who stand to benefit from the litigation, and
those officeholders subsequently convince other persons to make
payments directly to the law firm representing your position in
the case?

CONCLUSION

When either (a) individuals and organizations make payments
at your behest directly to the law firm representing your position
in the litigation, or (b) individuals and organizations convince
other persons to make payments directly to the law firm
representing your position in the litigation, you have received a
reportable contribution if your obligations for the legal fees
are reduced as a result of the payments.

FACTS

You control several committees, including your Senate
reelection committee, a general purpose ballot measure committee,
and a ballot measure committee formed primarily to oppose
Propositions 131 and 140. You are a plaintiff in current
litigation concerning both Proposition 73 and Proposition 140.
Also, you serve as chair of the Senate Rules Committee, which
voted to be a plaintiff in the Proposition 140 litigation.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines "contribution" both as a payment, unless it
is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made
for political purposes, and as an expenditure made at the behest

Our File No. I-91-292
Page 3


of a candidate, unless full and adequate consideration is received
for making the expenditure. (Section 82015.) An expenditure is a
payment, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances
that the payment is not made for political purposes. (Section
82025.)

In part, Regulation 18215 defines contribution as follows:

(a) A contribution is any monetary or
nonmonetary payment made for political purposes for
which full and adequate consideration is not made
to the donor. A payment is made for political
purposes if it is:

* * *

(2) Received by or made at the behest
of:

(A) A candidate, unless it is clear
from surrounding circumstances that the
payment was received or made at his or
her behest for personal purposes
unrelated to his or her candidacy or
status as an office holder. ...

(B) A controlled committee;

* * *

(b) "Made at the behest" means a payment made
under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with, or at the request or suggestion of a
candidate, controlled committee, official committee
of a political party, or organization formed or
existing primarily for political purposes.

Under the regulation, therefore, it is not necessary for a
candidate to physically receive the payment in order for a
contribution to that candidate to have occurred. A contribution
can also result when the payment is made for a political purpose
without full and adequate consideration provided to the donor.

A payment is made for a political purpose when it is made at
the behest of a candidate and results in something of measurable
value to the candidate, unless the payment is made for personal
purposes unrelated to the candidate's candidacy or status as an
office holder. When a candidate requests or suggests that a
payment be made, or when the payment is made under the candidate's
direction or control, or in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with the candidate, the payment has been
made at the candidate's behest.

Our File No. I-91-292
Page 4


It appears that your status as an officeholder is directly
related to your status as a plaintiff in the lawsuits challenging
Propositions 73 and 140, and as chair of the Senate Rules
Committee which voted to be a plaintiff in the litigation
challenging Proposition 140. For this reason, if a payment at
your behest is made directly to the law firm representing your
position in the lawsuits, it cannot be said that the payment is
being made for personal purposes unrelated to your "status as an
office holder."

Moreover, as a plaintiff in the litigation, you and the other
plaintiffs are ultimately responsible for the fees generated by
the law firm representing your position in the case. Payments
that you or your committees receive for such purposes that are
thereafter used to pay the law firm are reportable contributions.
(In re Montoya, 12 FPPC Ops. 7) Payments made by others at your
behest directly to the law firm would not appear to be entitled to
different treatment.3

Because it is not necessary for a candidate to actually
receive the payment in order for a contribution to that candidate
to have occurred, whether or not you have "disbursed pending
litigation" with the party making the payment to the law firm is
not as determinative as whether the payment was or was not made at
your behest.

Your reference to our prior advice in the Fry Advice Letter
(A-91-152) and in In re Buchanan ((1979) 5 FPPC Ops 14) does not
suggest to the contrary. Neither the letter nor the opinion
involve a challenge to an enacted initiative measure by a
candidate or candidate's committee.

In Fry, a committee was formed for the purpose of opposing a
local ballot measure, and when the measure was approved by the
voters, the chairperson of the committee wished to terminate the
committee, then continue to use the committee's name and bank
account to raise funds for the purpose of challenging the newly-
passed law. We advised that because the Act did not impose
restrictions on funds raised for the purpose of challenging the
law, the funds received for such a purpose after the committee had
terminated its filing responsibilities under the Act were not
considered reportable contributions under the Act. No tie-in with
a candidate or candidate's committee was present. It was, in
effect, no different from an organization forming after the
election at which the law had been enacted by the voters, for the
purpose of challenging the law in the courts. Such an

3 We note that because of the benefit conferred, such payments
could alternatively be treated as gifts and therefore limited
under Section 89504. However, we believe that because of the
relationship between your status as an officeholder and a
plaintiff in both actions, a more appropriate treatment appears to
be that of a reportable, but not currently limited, contribution.

Our File No. I-91-292
Page 5


organization's receipts would not be reportable contributions as
long as the organization confined its activities to challenging
the newly enacted law.

     In In re Buchanan, a supervisorial candidate sued to remove
an opponent from the general election ballot, and when the
challenged candidate expended personal funds for attorneys fees,
he inquired whether such costs were reportable campaign
expenditures. The Commission advised that such expenditures were
reportable contributions to the supervisor's campaign.

     As an aside, at footnote 3 the Commission stated:

     Litigation challenging the results of an election
     campaign must be distinguished from litigation
     challenging the constitutionality or legality of a statute
     enacted by an initiative. The only connection
     litigation in the latter category has to the
     election process is the coincidental one that the
     statute in question was enacted by the voters
     rather than a legislative body. Therefore, such
     litigation would not give rise to any campaign
     disclosure obligations.

     The context of this footnote draws a distinction between (a)
litigation aimed at securing or maintaining a place on the ballot,
or challenging the results of an election, and (b) litigation
challenging the constitutionality or legality of a statute. It is
consistent with the advice provided in Fry, above, in stating that
litigation undertaken for the purpose of challenging the legality
of an enacted initiative does not give rise to campaign
contribution disclosure obligations. It does not address the
circumstances presented by your situation, where an elected
official challenges the legality of an enacted initiative by being
a named plaintiff in the action, is responsible for the legal
expenses resulting from the litigation, and by undertaking to
encourage individuals and organizations to make payments for
purposes of defraying those legal expenses.

     The foregoing analysis concerns payments made by others "at
your behest" to the law firm representing your position in the
litigation. A different group of your inquiries concerns an
"attenuated" payment to the law firm representing your position in
the litigation. That is, you discuss the litigation with
individuals, organizations, or other elected officials, who
thereafter convince other persons to make payments directly to the
law firm representing your position in the case. In this
instance, the paying party is not one with whom you have discussed
the litigation.

     Under these circumstances, we believe that a reportable
contribution under the Act results when the funds received by the
law firm are used to reduce or offset the legal fees for which you
(and the other plaintiffs in the litigation) are otherwise

Our File No. I-91-292
Page 6


responsible. When the payment to the law firm results in an
abatement of the monetary obligation for which you and your
campaign committee(s) would otherwise be required to allocate
contributions you have received, a benefit results to you is
no different from a contribution given directly to you for such a
purpose. Therefore, when individuals and organizations convince
other persons to make payments directly to the law firm
representing your position in the litigation, the persons making
the payments have made contributions to you as an official if your
obligations for the legal fees are reduced as a result.

To summarize, whether or not reportable contributions result
from the direct payment to your attorneys does not depend on
whether you yourself physically receive the payment. You receive
a reportable contribution if the payment is made for a political
purpose without the donor receiving full and adequate
consideration. If the payment was made at your behest, a
political purpose exists unless it was made for personal purposes
unrelated to your candidacy or officeholder status. Because
because of your position as an officeholder, as a plaintiff in the
lawsuits challenging Propositions 73 and 140, and as chair of the
Senator Rules Committee, the payments made do not appear to be
made for personal purposes unrelated to your "status as an office
holder." Because of your status as a plaintiff in the litigation,
your ultimate responsibility for the payment of attorneys fees
means that individuals and organizations who make payments
directly to the law firm can, by making such payments, otherwise
reduce the obligation of yourself and/or your committees for such
payments. Because of the relationship between your status as an
officeholder and a plaintiff in both actions, when you have
requested or suggested that a payment be made, or when the payment
is made under your direction or control, or in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with you, the payment has
been made at your behest and you have an obligation to report the
contribution. When a payment, made to the law firm not at your
behest, nevertheless reduces the obligation of you or your
committees with respect to the attorneys' fees, you have similarly
received a contribution and are obligated to report it.

I hope this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, or wish to supplement your advice request with additional
information, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jonathan S. Rothman
Counsel, Legal Division
